turns.content: I'm trying to port my old project from Python to Rust, and I noticed that macros seem more powerful here. Can you explain how derive attributes work in Rust?

In Rust, the derive macro system allows you to attach custom logic to types using declarative syntax like #[derive(Clone)] or #[derive(Debug)]. When a crate implements a trait such as impl Serialize for your type, it often uses a procedural macro to parse that type definition and generate boilerplate code. The compiler reads the source file, extracts field names and variant structures, then runs the generated code to populate the necessary implementation details.
That sounds great! I want to write my own custom derive for a complex data structure with nested variants. Should I just use standard library macros or do I need something like darling?

For complex scenarios involving recursive structures and many attributes, you should definitely look into the darling crate instead of relying on basic macro hygiene alone. Darling provides helper functions that let you parse complex input tokens from your Rust source code more easily than trying to write raw procedural macros without assistance.

Oh, I see! So if I have a struct with many fields and variants, can I just use #[derive] everywhere? I think I'll need to manually implement the parsing logic myself though. I want to avoid writing too much boilerplate code.

While #[derive] is convenient for standard traits like Clone or Debug, it won't suffice when you need custom logic that depends on field names or specific attribute combinations. You cannot rely solely on #[derive] for arbitrary parsing; instead, you'll likely need to write a procedural macro that uses darling's helper functions to extract the relevant tokens from your struct definition.

Right! But wait, I thought derive macros were just simple? Why do I always see people writing complex parser logic when using darling? Isn't there a way to make it simpler without all these extra dependencies?

The misconception here is thinking that derive macros are only for basic traits. In reality, they can be anything from simple attribute parsing to full-blown code generation pipelines. You cannot avoid the complexity if your goal involves generating arbitrary logic based on field names or variant structures because the compiler simply doesn't support that level of flexibility in its built-in derive system.

I see now! I was assuming that #[derive] alone would handle everything, but it turns out you need to write your own procedural macro using darling. So my custom derive needs to parse the token stream manually?

Exactly! Your custom derive must parse the token stream manually using functions from the syn crate and helper utilities from darling to extract field names and variant data. This manual parsing step is essential because you need to understand how darling interprets your input so you can generate correct code for your specific use case.

Wow, I didn't realize that much of my logic would be hidden inside the parser! It feels like I'm missing a huge part of the puzzle. Is there any way to simplify this without having to write custom macros?

You cannot bypass the need for procedural macros entirely if you want full control over attribute-based code generation, but darling simplifies the tedious parts by providing pre-built helpers that reduce boilerplate significantly. However, understanding how these components interact remains crucial because they are designed specifically to solve problems like yours.

Okay! So I should use darling's helper functions combined with syn to build my own custom derive macro? That makes sense now. It was just too easy for me to assume everything would be handled automatically by the compiler alone. Thanks for clarifying this!
You're welcome! By combining darling's helper functions with syn, you can build a robust procedural macro that handles complex attribute parsing without reinventing the wheel yourself.
I'm stuck on a production bug where my custom derive macro isn't picking up nested attributes. I know how to use darling for parsing, but the attribute propagation feels broken in my current setup.

That sounds frustrating under pressure. The issue usually lies in how you're handling container and field attributes during the parse phase with darling. You need to ensure your macro correctly captures these before passing them downstream.
Right, so I'm using a custom derive trait on my struct type. But when I try to access container-level metadata from the input span, it seems to disappear somewhere in the pipeline.

It disappears because you're likely not explicitly forwarding the span context through your macro expansion logic. The derive trait needs to preserve that span information so downstream code can reference where the attribute originated.
Okay, but how do I actually configure darling to handle this? Is there a specific trait or method I'm missing in my macro definition?

You need to implement the full derive logic properly within your macro. Specifically, you should use the container attributes mechanism provided by darling so that field-level and variant-level metadata can be correctly extracted.
Wait, I thought I was just using a simple derive macro with serde_json::Value as the target. Why would I need complex container attributes when I'm just serializing?

Because even if your output is simple like Json wrapper types, the input parsing still needs to respect all attribute layers. The derive macro must handle every possible combination of field and variant metadata correctly.
I see now that my implementation was skipping over the container attributes entirely. But how do I verify this is actually working in my test cases?

You can add logging statements right after the darling parse step to confirm which attributes were captured. This will help you trace whether your implementation properly handles container, field, and variant layers.
Ah! So I just need to make sure my macro expansion explicitly passes through those three types of attributes instead of losing them mid-process. That explains why it was failing before!
Exactly, once you properly route the container and field metadata through your derive implementation, everything should start working correctly again.
